sentence_text: In the absence of air resistance, heavier and lighter objects fall at the same rate because gravity accelerates all masses equally.
A heavier object does feel a larger gravitational force, but it also has more inertia, meaning it resists changes in motion more.
These two effects balance out.
Newton’s second law says acceleration equals force divided by mass.
Since gravitational force is proportional to mass, the mass cancels out, leaving the same acceleration for all objects.
That is why, in a vacuum, a feather and a hammer dropped together fall side by side and hit the ground at the same time.
In the absence of air resistance, heavier and lighter objects fall at the same rate because gravity accelerates all masses equally.
A heavier object does feel a larger gravitational force, but it also has more inertia, meaning it resists changes in motion more.
These two effects balance out.
Newton’s second law says acceleration equals force divided by mass.
Since gravitational force is proportional to mass, the mass cancels out, leaving the same acceleration for all objects.
That is why, in a vacuum, a feather and a hammer dropped together fall side by side and hit the ground at the same time.
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, and velocity includes both speed and direction.
In circular motion, even if the speed stays constant, the direction of motion is continuously changing, so the velocity is changing.
This means there is an acceleration, called centripetal acceleration, which always points toward the center of the circle.
The relevant principle is that any change in velocity requires a net force (Newton’s second law).
For example, when a car turns at constant speed, you feel pushed sideways because your direction is changing, indicating an inward acceleration toward the center of the turn.
Acceleration is defined as any change in velocity, and velocity is a vector, meaning it has both magnitude and direction.
Even if your speed stays constant around a circle, your direction of motion is continuously changing, so your velocity vector is constantly changing.
That change in velocity per unit time is acceleration, called centripetal acceleration, always pointing toward the center of the circle.
Think of swinging a ball on a string: the string pulls the ball inward, constantly redirecting it without speeding it up.
Without that inward pull, the ball would fly off in a straight line, as Newton's first law predicts.
When magnetic flux through a loop changes, an induced current appears whose direction opposes that change—this is Lenz’s law.
The deeper reason is conservation of energy: if the induced current reinforced the change, the system would amplify itself without any external work, creating energy from nothing.
Instead, the induced current produces its own magnetic field that resists the change, so an external agent must do work to alter the flux.
For example, pushing a magnet toward a conducting loop induces a current whose magnetic field repels the magnet, making it feel harder to push—your mechanical work becomes electrical energy.

When a magnetic flux through a conducting loop changes, the loop responds by driving a current that creates its own magnetic field to resist that change — this is Faraday's law combined with Lenz's law.

The deeper reason is energy conservation: if the induced current reinforced the change instead of opposing it, the growing magnetic field would induce a stronger current, which would grow the field further, generating energy from nothing, violating conservation of energy.
Think of it like mechanical inertia — just as a stationary object resists being pushed, a stable magnetic flux resists being disturbed.
Nature always pushes back against the disturbance, never amplifying it freely.
Wave speed, frequency, and wavelength are linked by the equation v = fλ, where v is wave speed, f is frequency, and λ is wavelength.
In a given medium, the wave speed is usually fixed by the medium’s properties, so frequency and wavelength must adjust to keep their product constant.
If the frequency increases, the wavelength decreases proportionally.
For example, if you shake a rope faster, the ripples become more closely spaced rather than moving faster along the rope.
The key principle is that the medium sets the speed, so changes in frequency are balanced by opposite changes in wavelength.
All waves obey the relationship v = fλ, where v is wave speed, f is frequency, and λ is wavelength.
In a given medium, the wave speed is fixed by the physical properties of that medium — for example, sound travels at roughly 343 m/s in air regardless of the source.
This means if you increase the frequency, the wavelength must decrease proportionally to keep the speed constant.
Think of a jump rope being shaken faster: the waves come more frequently, but each individual wave crest is shorter, while the speed at which disturbances travel along the rope stays the same.
In free fall without air resistance, all objects accelerate downward at the same rate regardless of mass.
A heavier object does experience a larger gravitational force, but it also has proportionally greater inertia.
Newton’s second law says acceleration equals force divided by mass.
Since gravity provides a force equal to mass times g, the mass cancels out, leaving the same acceleration for all objects.
This means a hammer and a feather would fall together in a vacuum.
The differences we see in everyday life come from air resistance, not from mass itself.
An object in uniform circular motion can have constant speed but still accelerate because velocity is a vector with both magnitude and direction.
In circular motion, the direction of velocity changes continuously, so velocity changes even when speed does not.
Acceleration measures that change in velocity, so it is nonzero and points toward the center of the circle.
Its magnitude is a_c = v^2/r (or r*omega^2).
A tighter turn (smaller r) or higher speed increases this inward acceleration.
Faraday's law says a changing magnetic flux induces an emf.
The minus sign in Faraday's law expresses Lenz's law: the induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that created it.
This opposition is a consistency condition with energy conservation.
If induced current reinforced the original change, flux would grow without extra work.
Instead, induced effects resist the change, so external work is required to keep changing the field.
Wave speed, frequency, and wavelength satisfy v = f lambda.
In one medium, speed is set mainly by medium properties, so if frequency increases then wavelength must decrease to keep v the same.
Frequency is set by the source, while wavelength adjusts in response to speed in that medium.
This is why high-frequency sound has shorter wavelength than low-frequency sound when both travel through the same air.
